results.title: Sudden uptick in weaver complaints - General Discussion - Overwatch Forums
results.content:
# Sudden uptick in weaver complaints

I know since his release he has had people talking (mostly people that don’t like the fact they’re getting saved by his pull), but what is with the recent uptick in extra hate and wanting a rework?

He’s like, completely fine in his current state. More people could benefit from trying to run the beaver comp. Where is it coming from?

Honestly, it feels like the same amount to me. But I haven’t been suuuuper active here

Because lifeweaver is in a decentish spot, people are probably seeing him a bit more, though. And I think, as a certified Lifeweaver expert, there still aren’t a lot of good lifeweaver players.

Powered by Discourse, best viewed with JavaScript enabled

results.title: 5 Easy To Fix New Weaver Mistakes - Warped Fibers
results.content:
Here is the thing: the weavers that take their time and pay attention to what they are doing probably weave faster!

There is a common saying: slow is fast and that applies to weaving perfectly.

It can be incredibly frustrating to finish setting up your weaving only to notice that you threaded your heddles wrong or you lost your X on your simple frame loom, especially if it is at the very beginning!

If you take an extra few seconds to double check as you go, then you won’t have to undo all the work you just did. This will save you both a lot of time and a lot of frustration.

Still made a threading mistake? It happens. Learn how to fix threading mistakes here.

This idea is also easily applied to the actual weaving process. [...] So let’s do that:

## Jump To:

 Not Checking Your Work As You Go
 Not Minding Your Selvedges
 Not Making Samples
 Not Beating Hard Enough (Or Beating Too Hard)
 Not Weaving In Your Tails
  + Overall Biggest New Weaver Mistake = Not Taking Your Time

Need help planning your weaving project? Stuck trying to figure out how much yarn you need? What the h&^$ is WPI? Check out my e-book!

 Facebook
 Twitter
 Pinterest

## Not Checking Your Work As You Go

Weaving is a fluid motion.

Over, under, over, under. It is easy to get lost in the repetition. You start to zone out when setting up your floor loom or frame loom or you’re trying to go really fast because all you want to do is get to the “good” part! [...] I don’t want you to think that you have to have an eagle eye on your weaving as you create and can’t relax. Weaving is supposed to be relaxing. As with most things, it is important to find a balance. If that means slowing down a little bit too? It is ok to give yourself the permission to do that.

Take a breath, stretch, and check your weaving as you go.

## Not Minding Your Selvedges

 Facebook
 Twitter
 Pinterest

Selvedges are one of the hardest things for new (and experienced!) weavers to wrap their heads around. We have all seen or made weavings that were supposed to be rectangles, but turned into trapezoids…

Sometimes in an attempt to fix this, you might even leave TOO much weft and get lumpy looped selvedges.

results.title: REVIEW: Bitter, Sweet: How to Heal Yourself When Your Family Is Broken by Stephanie Weaver | Hippocampus Magazine
results.content: I never thought I’d say a book about CSA and family dysfunction was compulsively readable, but it was. Weaver’s writing is approachable, and secure in the telling. Most of the events that Weaver describes happened over 30 years ago; the most recent ones are nearly a decade old. Meaning that Weaver has clearly had time to process and analyze what has happened to her without drawing directly from the emotion itself. Write from the scar, not the wound is how the popular adage goes, and Weaver puts that on full display here. [...] At the outset, Weaver makes it clear that this is her personal journey. Her story is neatly contrasted with that of her sister, who remained estranged from their parents until their deaths. Weaver does not judge her sister, nor anyone who decides to stay estranged from family members. She is simply speaking to her experience, and recounts the tools she used to reestablish contact once she realized that was what she wanted. Weaver is suggestive without being prescriptive: she describes the therapies and alternative modalities she used without false promises that they will work for everyone. [...] This book can be for anyone who has dealt with family estrangement, and has an interest in reopening those lines of communication, who felt as Weaver did that ‘something was missing.’ I would recommend reading trigger warnings, as the book does contain some graphic descriptions, especially when Weaver describes the abuse she and her siblings endured. It is not self-help per se, but an offering of how one person managed to regain contact. The book can serve as a road map, or merely an opportunity for reflection. While unusual in the self-help space, the subtitle implies the book is more self-help than it actually is.

results.title: How to Fix a Broken Warp Thread - Gist Yarn
results.content:
# How to Fix a Broken Warp Thread

by Amanda Rataj

Do you remember your first broken warp thread? I do - I was at my parents house alone, working happily on one of my mum’s looms when a warp end snapped. I had no idea what to do, and, if I’m being honest, I was too impatient to Google it effectively. I had been having so much fun! If I had only known how simple it was to fix a broken warp thread - I could have been back at it in only a few minutes.

Regardless of how careful a weaver you are, we all break warp threads. Sometimes it’s because we’ve beamed our warp funny or we’ve been packing our weft a bit too enthusiastically. Whatever the reason is, a broken warp thread does NOT spell disaster (or ugliness!) for your finished textile - you likely have all the tools to do it already.
